subject: Company Law
module_title: Corporate Decision-Making: Resolutions, Meetings and Board Governance
lesson_title: AGMs, EGMs and General Meeting Procedure

questions.question: Under s.175 CA 2014, which type of Irish private company is EXEMPT from the obligation to hold an annual general meeting (AGM)?

options.A: All private limited companies (LTDs) are exempt from the AGM obligation
options.B: Only single-member companies are exempt from holding AGMs
options.C: Private limited companies (LTDs) are exempt; DACs, CLGs, PLCs, and unlimited companies must hold AGMs
options.D: Companies with fewer than 5 shareholders are exempt from the AGM requirement

questions.explanation: Under s.175 CA 2014, private limited companies (LTDs) — the most common Irish company form — are not required to hold an AGM. This is a significant departure from the pre-2014 position. DACs, CLGs, PLCs, and unlimited companies retain the obligation to hold AGMs under s.176.

questions.question: Under s.176 CA 2014, a DAC or other company type required to hold an AGM must do so within what time period, and what business must be transacted at the AGM?

options.A: Within 6 months of the financial year end; mandatory business includes consideration of accounts, directors' report, and auditors' report
options.B: Within 9 months of the financial year end; mandatory business is purely at the directors' discretion
options.C: Within 15 months of the previous AGM (and within 9 months of the financial year end in the first instance); mandatory business includes considering the accounts and reports
options.D: Within 12 months of the previous AGM; no mandatory business is specified — the board determines the agenda

questions.explanation: Under s.176 CA 2014, the AGM must be held within 15 months of the last AGM (and within 9 months of the financial year end for the first AGM). The mandatory business includes consideration of the company's statutory financial statements, the directors' report, and the auditors' report.

questions.question: Under s.177 CA 2014, shareholders holding what minimum percentage of paid-up voting capital can requisition an Extraordinary General Meeting (EGM), and within what period must the directors convene it?

options.A: 5% of paid-up voting capital; directors must convene within 21 days
options.B: 10% of paid-up voting capital; directors must convene within 21 days of requisition, and the meeting must be held within 2 months of the requisition
options.C: 15% of paid-up voting capital; directors have 28 days to convene
options.D: 25% of paid-up voting capital; directors must convene within 14 days of requisition

questions.explanation: Under s.177 CA 2014, members holding not less than 10% of paid-up voting capital may requisition an EGM. The directors must convene the meeting within 21 days of receipt of the requisition, and the meeting must be held within 2 months. Failure by the directors to convene entitles the requisitionists to convene it themselves.

questions.question: If the directors fail to convene an EGM following a valid s.177 requisition, what remedy do the requisitioning shareholders have under CA 2014?

options.A: They must apply to court for a mandatory order compelling the directors to hold the meeting
options.B: The requisitionists themselves may convene the meeting at the company's expense within 3 months of their requisition
options.C: The requisitionists may apply to the CRO to appoint an independent administrator to hold the meeting
options.D: The shareholders may automatically pass the proposed resolution by written resolution under s.161 without a meeting

questions.explanation: Under s.177(3) CA 2014, where the directors fail to convene an EGM within 21 days of a valid requisition, the requisitionists may themselves convene the meeting within 3 months. The reasonable expenses of doing so are recoverable from the company, and the directors responsible for the failure are personally liable to repay the company.

questions.question: Pedley v Inland Waterways Association [1977] (England, applied in Ireland) addressed the right of members to include items on a general meeting agenda. What principle does this case illustrate?

options.A: Members may only add items to the agenda that are specified in the articles as ordinary business for the AGM
options.B: Members who requisition a meeting for a specific purpose may not discuss matters beyond the stated purpose without unanimous shareholder consent
options.C: The right to add business to a general meeting agenda belongs exclusively to the board — members have no statutory right to include items on the agenda unless the articles so provide
options.D: Members holding the requisite threshold (typically 10%) may add items to the AGM agenda by serving notice on the company

questions.explanation: Pedley v Inland Waterways Association [1977] establishes that members meeting the requisition threshold may require the company to include items of business on the AGM agenda. This right exists separately from the right to requisition a full EGM. Under CA 2014, s.181 provides similar rights for members to add resolutions to AGM agendas.

questions.question: The Companies (Corporate Governance, Enforcement and Regulatory Provisions) Act 2024 inserted s.176A into CA 2014, permitting virtual or hybrid general meetings. What conditions or safeguards apply under s.176A?

options.A: Virtual meetings are permitted without restriction — all types of meetings may be held fully virtually at the board's discretion
options.B: Virtual or hybrid meetings are permitted for all company types subject to the articles not prohibiting them; the technology used must allow all participants to see, hear, and speak in real time, and members must be able to exercise their rights effectively
options.C: Virtual meetings are limited to private limited companies only and require a unanimous shareholder resolution approving virtual meeting format each year
options.D: s.176A only permits virtual meeting attendance for shareholders outside Ireland — quorum must always include at least one member physically present at a designated location

questions.explanation: s.176A CA 2014 (inserted by the 2024 Act) permits virtual and hybrid general meetings for all company types, subject to the articles not prohibiting them. The technology must ensure all participants can participate fully — see, hear, and speak in real time — to preserve the quality of participation that a physical meeting provides.

questions.question: Short notice consent for a general meeting requires agreement from a specific proportion of shareholders. What proportion must agree to short notice for: (a) a meeting involving an ordinary resolution; and (b) a meeting to pass a special resolution?

options.A: (a) All shareholders; (b) 90% of shareholders by voting rights
options.B: (a) A majority of shareholders by voting rights; (b) 75% by voting rights
options.C: (a) 90% of shareholders by voting rights; (b) 90% of shareholders by voting rights — the same threshold applies to both
options.D: (a) 51% by voting rights; (b) 75% by voting rights

questions.explanation: Under CA 2014, short notice consent requires 90% of shareholders by voting rights for both ordinary resolution meetings and special resolution meetings. The single 90% threshold applies regardless of the type of resolution being proposed (unless the articles set a higher threshold). This is a change from the pre-2014 position where 95% was required for special resolutions.

questions.question: A proxy votes on a poll at a general meeting. The appointing member subsequently changes their mind and attends the meeting in person. Does the member's physical attendance automatically revoke the proxy for voting purposes?

options.A: No — a proxy once appointed is irrevocable until the conclusion of the meeting for which it was issued
options.B: Yes — a member's personal attendance at a meeting automatically revokes any proxy previously appointed for that meeting
options.C: It depends — if the member gives the chairperson written notice of revocation before the proxy votes, it is revoked; otherwise the proxy vote stands
options.D: No — the proxy must first be formally revoked in writing by the member; attendance alone does not revoke a proxy

questions.explanation: The general rule under Irish company law is that a member's personal attendance at a general meeting automatically revokes any proxy they had previously appointed for that meeting. The member's presence supersedes the proxy. However, if the proxy has already voted on a poll before the member attends, the vote may already be counted.

questions.question: Under CA 2014, a poll at a general meeting gives each member one vote per share held (or as otherwise provided in the articles). A show of hands gives one vote per member present. When can a member DEMAND a poll?

options.A: Only if the chairperson agrees to order a poll — members have no right to demand a poll as of right
options.B: A poll may be demanded by members prescribed in the articles or, in default of such provision, by not fewer than five members having the right to vote, or by members holding at least 10% of total voting rights
options.C: Any single member may demand a poll on any resolution at any general meeting
options.D: A poll can only be demanded before the show of hands takes place — if a show of hands result has been declared, it is final and a poll cannot be ordered

questions.explanation: Under s.188 CA 2014, a poll may be demanded by: not fewer than five members having the right to vote; members holding not less than 10% of total voting rights; or the chairperson. The articles may provide for additional poll demand rights. A poll demanded on the election of a chairperson or adjournment can be held immediately.

questions.question: The court has jurisdiction to order a meeting to be held under s.179 CA 2014. When does this jurisdiction arise, and what safeguards apply?

options.A: The court may order a meeting only where the company is insolvent and the liquidator cannot convene the meeting voluntarily
options.B: The court may order a meeting where it is impractical to call or conduct a meeting in the manner prescribed by the articles or the Act. The court may give such directions as it thinks expedient including as to the calling, holding and conduct of the meeting
options.C: The court may only intervene in meeting procedure where a shareholder makes a written complaint to the CRO and the CRO refers the matter to the court
options.D: The court may order a meeting only where the requisite quorum cannot be obtained because of deliberate obstruction by certain shareholders

questions.explanation: s.179 CA 2014 empowers the court to call, hold and conduct meetings where it is impractical to do so in the prescribed manner — for example, where deadlock or shareholder misconduct makes it impossible. The court may set the quorum (including as low as one), appoint a chairperson, and give directions on the conduct of the meeting.
